notes_md:
**dì** = your mom’s younger sister (your maternal aunt, younger than your mom).

It’s a family noun, usually used without a classifier in everyday speech: just **dì**. To be clear whose aunt, say **dì + của + someone** or use a possessive like **dì mình, dì em**.  

Don’t confuse it with **cô** (your dad’s younger sister). Vietnamese is very specific about family roles, so picking the right word matters.  

You can also attach a name after it to address her directly, like **dì + Name** (used like “Aunt + Name”).
notes:
dì = your mom’s younger sister (your maternal aunt, younger than your mom).

It’s a family noun, usually used without a classifier in everyday speech: just dì. To be clear whose aunt, say dì + của + someone or use a possessive like dì mình, dì em.  

Don’t confuse it with cô (your dad’s younger sister). Vietnamese is very specific about family roles, so picking the right word matters.  

You can also attach a name after it to address her directly, like dì + Name (used like “Aunt + Name”).
exampleSentences: Cuối tuần này, em sẽ đi thăm dì ở thành phố.
Dì thường nấu những món ăn ngon khi anh đến chơi.
Mỗi dịp lễ Tết, gia đình em đều sang nhà dì chúc Tết.
exampleSentencesTranslated: This weekend, I will visit my aunt in the city.
My aunt often cooks delicious food when I come over.
Every holiday, my family visits my aunt to wish her a happy new year.
